Project: Data-driven Insights using Python: FoodHub Data Analysis¶

Problem Statement¶

Context¶

The number of restaurants in New York is increasing day by day. Lots of students and busy professionals rely on those restaurants due to their hectic lifestyles. An online food delivery service is a great option for them. It provides them with good food from their favorite restaurants. A food aggregator company FoodHub, offers access to multiple restaurants through a single smartphone app. The app allows the restaurants to receive a direct online order from a customer. The app assigns a delivery person from the company to pick up the order after it is confirmed by the restaurant. The delivery person then uses the map to reach the restaurant and waits for the food package. Once the food package is handed over to the delivery person, he/she confirm the pick-up in the app and travel to the customer's location to deliver the food. The delivery person confirms the drop-off in the app after delivering the food package to the customer. The customer can rate the order in the app. The food aggregator earns money by collecting a fixed margin of the delivery order from the restaurants.

Objective¶

The food aggregator company has stored the data of the different orders made by the registered customers in its online portal. They want to analyze the data to get a fair idea about the demand for different restaurants, which will help them enhance their customer experience. Suppose you are hired as a Data Scientist in this company, and the Data Science team has shared some of the key questions that need to be answered. Perform the data analysis to find answers to these questions that will help the company improve its business.

Data Description¶

The data contains different data related to a food order. The detailed data dictionary is given below.

  • order_id: Unique ID of the order
  • customer_id: ID of the customer who ordered the food
  • restaurant_name: Name of the restaurant
  • cuisine_type: Cuisine ordered by the customer
  • cost_of_the_order: Cost of the order
  • day_of_the_week: Indicates whether the order is placed on a weekday or weekend (The weekday is from Monday to Friday and the weekend is Saturday and Sunday)
  • rating: Rating given by the customer out of 5
  • food_preparation_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the restaurant to prepare the food. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the restaurant's order confirmation and the delivery person's pick-up confirmation.
  • delivery_time: Time (in minutes) taken by the delivery person to deliver the food package. This is calculated by taking the difference between the timestamps of the delivery person's pick-up confirmation and drop-off information

Let us start by importing the required libraries¶

import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Import libraries for data visualization
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

FoodPreparation Time:

Cost of Order

Delivery Time

  • The delivery time ranges from a minimum of 15 mins to a max of 33 mins, with an average food preparation time is approximately 27 minutes.

Question 9: What percentage of the orders cost more than 20 dollars? [2 marks]¶

In [195]:
# Create a new DataFrame that only contains orders with 'cost_of_the_order' greater than 20
df_greater_than_20 = df[df['cost_of_the_order']>20]
# Print the total number of orders that cost more than 20 dollars
print('The number of total orders that cost more then 20 dollars is:', df_greater_than_20.shape[0])
# Calculate the percentage of orders that cost more than 20 dollars
# df_greater_than_20.shape[0] gives the number of orders > $20
# df.shape[0] gives the total number of orders in the original DataFrame
percentage = (df_greater_than_20.shape[0] / df.shape[0]) * 100
# Print the percentage of orders that cost more than 20 dollars, rounded to 2 decimal places
print(f'The percentage of orders that cost more than 20 dollars is: {percentage:.2f}%')

The number of total orders that cost more then 20 dollars is: 555
The percentage of orders that cost more than 20 dollars is: 29.24%

Observations:¶

  1. There are 555 orders that cost more then 20 dollars
  2. The percentage of orders that cost more than 20 dollars is: 29.24%

#scatter plot of Customer orders for each cuisine_type
plt.figure(figsize=(8,6))
sns.scatterplot(data=df, x='cuisine_type', y='customer_id', hue='cuisine_type', palette='Dark2')
# Rotate x-axis labels for readability
plt.xticks(rotation=60)
# Add labels and title
plt.title("Customer Orders by Cuisine Type")
plt.ylabel("Customer")
plt.xlabel("Cuisine Type")
# Adjust legend
plt.legend(title='Cuisine Type', bbox_to_anchor=(1.05, 1), loc='upper left')
# Show the plot
plt.show()

  1. Japanese, American, Indian, Italian, and Chinese cuisines are the most popular choices with the highest frequency of orders across the customer base.

  2. Korean, French, Spanish, and Vietnamese cuisines have fewer data points compared to other cuisines, indicating they are more specialty cuisines ordered by a smaller subset of customers, reflecting lower popularity or limited availability.

In [241]:
#  Boxplot: Cost of Order by Cuisine Type
plt.figure(figsize=(12,6))
sns.boxplot(data=df, x='cuisine_type', y='cost_of_the_order', hue='cuisine_type', palette='Dark2')
plt.xticks(rotation = 60)
plt.show()
  1. the most popular cuisines(American/Japanese) provide the most stable revenue stream across all price points.
  2. American(median 14.12 dollars, max 33.18 dollars), Japanese(median 14.07 dollars, max 33.37 dollars), Italian (median 14.48 dollars,max 33.03 dollars), Chinese (median 14.12 dollars, max 34.19 dollars)- These appear across all price ranges.
In [242]:
# Relationship between food preparation time and cuisine type
plt.figure(figsize=(15,7))
sns.boxplot(x = "cuisine_type", y = "food_preparation_time", data = df, palette = 'PuBu', hue = "cuisine_type")
plt.xticks(rotation = 60)
plt.show()
  1. Most cusines have a similar preparation time range between 20 and 35 minutes.
  2. Most cuisines have a median preparation time centered between 25 and 28 mins.
  3. Italian, Thai, and Japanese cuisines have the higher median preparation times, appearing closer to the 28-minutes .
In [204]:
#Rating vs. Food Preparation & Delivery Time
#  Create bins for delivery time
df['delivery_time_bins'] = pd.cut(df['delivery_time'], bins=[15, 20, 25, 30, 35])

#  Create a pivot table showing the count of orders for each rating and time bin
heatmap_data = df.pivot_table(index='rating', observed=False,
columns='delivery_time_bins',
values='cost_of_the_order',
aggfunc='count')

# Plot the heatmap
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.heatmap(heatmap_data, annot=True, fmt='g', cmap='YlGnBu')
plt.title('Heatmap: Order Frequency by Rating and Delivery Time')
plt.xlabel('Delivery Time Range (min)')
plt.ylabel('Rating')
plt.show()
  1. The highest orders occure in 25-30 min time range who did not provide a rating value is 281.
  2. Customers who actually provided a 5- star rating has a delivery time between 25-30 mins.

Question 13: The company wants to provide a promotional offer in the advertisement of the restaurants. The condition to get the offer is that the restaurants must have a rating count of more than 50 and the average rating should be greater than 4. Find the restaurants fulfilling the criteria to get the promotional offer. [3 marks]¶

In [243]:
# Filter out missing ratings
df_ratings = df[df['rating'] != 'Not given'].copy()

# Convert rating column datatype to int
df_ratings['rating'] = df_ratings['rating'].astype(int)

# Group by restaurant to get count and average rating
rating_count = df_ratings.groupby('restaurant_name').agg(rating_count=('rating', 'count'),average_rating=('rating', 'mean'))

# Filter for promotional eligibility
eligible_restaurants = rating_count[(rating_count['rating_count'] > 50) & (rating_count['average_rating'] > 4)]

#eligible_restaurants_sorted = eligible_restaurants.sort_values(by='rating_count', ascending=False)
eligible_restaurants_sorted = eligible_restaurants.sort_values(by='rating_count',ascending=False).reset_index()
eligible_restaurants_sorted
Out[243]:
restaurant_name rating_count average_rating
0 Shake Shack 133 4.278195
1 The Meatball Shop 84 4.511905
2 Blue Ribbon Sushi 73 4.219178
3 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken 64 4.328125

Observations:¶

The restaurants that are eligible to get the promotional offers are Shake Shack,The Meatball Shop,Blue Ribbon Sushi,3 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken

Question 14: The company charges the restaurant 25% on the orders having cost greater than 20 dollars and 15% on the orders having cost greater than 5 dollars. Find the net revenue generated by the company across all orders. [3 marks]¶

In [244]:
#define a function to calculate net revenue
def cal_rev(x):
  if x > 20: #'x' is order cost if >20 then apply 25% charge on the order
    return x * 0.25
  elif x > 5: #'x' is order cost if >20 then apply 25% charge on the order
    return x * 0.15
  else:
    return x * 0
df['net_revenue'] = df['cost_of_the_order'].apply(cal_rev)
df.head()
Out[244]:
order_id customer_id restaurant_name cuisine_type cost_of_the_order day_of_the_week rating food_preparation_time delivery_time delivery_time_bins net_revenue
0 1477147 337525 Hangawi Korean 30.75 Weekend Not given 25 20 (15.0, 20.0] 7.6875
1 1477685 358141 Blue Ribbon Sushi Izakaya Japanese 12.08 Weekend Not given 25 23 (20.0, 25.0] 1.8120
2 1477070 66393 Cafe Habana Mexican 12.23 Weekday 5 23 28 (25.0, 30.0] 1.8345
3 1477334 106968 Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken American 29.20 Weekend 3 25 15 NaN 7.3000
4 1478249 76942 Dirty Bird to Go American 11.59 Weekday 4 25 24 (20.0, 25.0] 1.7385
In [245]:
total_revenue = df['net_revenue'].sum()
print('The net revenue is around', round(total_revenue, 2), 'dollars')

The net revenue is around 6166.3 dollars

Observations:¶

  • The net revenue generated by the company across alll orders is $6166.3

Question 15: The company wants to analyze the total time required to deliver the food. What percentage of orders take more than 60 minutes to get delivered from the time the order is placed? (The food has to be prepared and then delivered.) [2 marks]¶

In [246]:
df['total_time'] = df['food_preparation_time'] + df['delivery_time']

# Calculate the number of orders that take more than 60 minutes
orders_over_60_min = df[df['total_time'] > 60]
num_orders_over_60_min = orders_over_60_min.shape[0]

# Calculate the total number of orders
total_orders = df.shape[0]

# Calculate the percentage
percentage_over_60_min = (num_orders_over_60_min / total_orders) * 100

print(f"The mean total time for food preparation and delivery is: {df['total_time'].mean():.2f} minutes")
print(f"Number of orders taking more than 60 minutes: {num_orders_over_60_min}")
print(f"Percentage of orders taking more than 60 minutes: {percentage_over_60_min:.2f}%")

The mean total time for food preparation and delivery is: 51.53 minutes
Number of orders taking more than 60 minutes: 200
Percentage of orders taking more than 60 minutes: 10.54%

Observations:¶

The mean total time for food preparation and delivery is: 51.53 minutes Number of orders taking more than 60 minutes: 200 Percentage of orders taking more than 60 minutes: 10.54%

Question 16: The company wants to analyze the delivery time of the orders on weekdays and weekends. How does the mean delivery time vary during weekdays and weekends? [2 marks]¶

In [247]:
#df.describe()

# Get the mean delivery time on weekdays and print it
print('The mean delivery time on weekdays is around',
      round(df[df['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekday']['delivery_time'].mean()),
     'minutes')
print('The mean delivery time on weekdays is around',
      round(df[df['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekend']['delivery_time'].mean()),
     'minutes')

The mean delivery time on weekdays is around 28 minutes
The mean delivery time on weekdays is around 22 minutes

Observations:¶

The average delivery time is approximately 28 minutes on weekdays and 22 minutes on weekends.

In [248]:
# Total orders on weekdays and weekends
total_weekday_orders = len(df[df['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekday'])
total_weekend_orders = len(df[df['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekend'])

# Orders with delivery time > 30 mins
long_weekday_orders = len(df[(df['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekday') & (df['delivery_time'] > 30)])
long_weekend_orders = len(df[(df['day_of_the_week'] == 'Weekend') & (df['delivery_time'] > 30)])

# Calculate percentages
percentage_long_weekday = (long_weekday_orders / total_weekday_orders) * 100
percentage_long_weekend = (long_weekend_orders / total_weekend_orders) * 100

print(f"Percentage of weekday orders with delivery time > 30 mins: {percentage_long_weekday:.2f}%")
print(f"Percentage of weekend orders with delivery time > 30 mins: {percentage_long_weekend:.2f}%")

Percentage of weekday orders with delivery time > 30 mins: 27.24%
Percentage of weekend orders with delivery time > 30 mins: 0.00%

Conclusion and Business Recommendations¶

Question 17: What are your conclusions from the analysis? What recommendations would you like to share to help improve the business? (You can use cuisine type and feedback ratings to drive your business recommendations.) [6 marks]¶

Conclusions¶

  • A few restaurants (Shake Shack, The Meatball Shop, Blue Ribbon Sushi, Blue Ribbon Fried Chicken) and cuisines (American, Japanese, Italian, Chinese) drive most of the orders and revenue.

  • Specialty cuisines (Thai, French, Southern, Korean, Spanish, Vietnamese) have very low demand and contribute minimally to revenue.

  • Preparation and delivery times are generally consistent, with most orders prepared in 25–28 minutes and delivered in 25 -30 minutes.

  • Faster delivery times correlate with higher customer ratings.

  • Customer engagement varies: a small segment is highly loyal, while most customers place only one order.

  • Many orders (736) have missing ratings, indicating a gap in feedback collection.

  • Popular cuisines generate stable revenue across all price points (4.47 dollars- 35.41 dollars).

  • Cuisines with higher median preparation times (Italian, Thai, Japanese) may require monitoring to avoid delays during peak hours.

  • There is potential to increase revenue by encouraging repeat orders through loyalty programs or targeted incentives.

Business Recommendations¶

  • FoodHub should focus on high-performing restaurants and popular cuisines (Shake Shack, The Meatball Shop, Blue Ribbon restaurants; American, Japanese, Italian, Chinese) for targeted marketing and promotions.

  • Promote specialty cuisines (Thai, French, Southern, Korean, Spanish, Vietnamese) strategically through limited-time offers, combo deals, or awareness campaigns.

  • Maintain preparation and delivery efficiency, ensuring most orders are delivered within 25–30 minutes to maximize customer satisfaction.

  • Encourage customers to provide ratings through reminders, simplified feedback, or small incentives.

  • FoodHub should introduce loyalty programs or targeted incentives to increase repeat orders.

  • FoodHub should monitor cuisines with higher median preparation times (Italian, Thai, Japanese) to prevent delays during peak hours.

  • Leverage popular cuisines across all price ranges to maintain a stable revenue stream.

  • Use data-driven insights to tailor menu offerings, promotions, and pricing strategies effectively.

  • Address weekday delivery delays: approximately 27% of weekday orders take longer than 30 minutes, so operational improvements or additional staffing may be needed to reduce delays.
